(ISO) BRUSSELS, BELGIUM

TELEX OMSSA 2637 • FACSIMILE 32 (2) 646 47 29

OUR FILE NUMBER

128,492-001

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

(415) 984-8741

February
8th
1991

Margaret Ellison
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:  California Association of County
     Treasurers and Tax Collectors

Dear Ms. Ellison:

     On behalf of the California Association of County
Treasurers and Tax Collectors (the "Association"), we are
seeking your advice with respect to the characterization of
certain gifts made by the Association from Association funds
to its members who are county treasurers and tax collectors.

     The Association is a 501(c)(6) organization under
the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended. The purpose of
the Association, as provided in its Articles of Association,
is to promote the general interests of the Active Members and
the respective Counties they represent, to strive for higher
professional standards and, through the exchange of
information and ideas, stimulate a friendly and cooperative
spirit among the membership.

     The Association has four classes of membership: (1)
Active Members - any duly elected or appointed County
Treasurer and/or Tax Collector within the State of California;
(2) Associate Members - any officer, partner, manager or
representative of a firm having an active interest in and
ability to contribute to the purposes of the Association; (3)
Life Members - any retired Active Member elected for such
membership by a majority vote of the members; and (4)
Honorary Members - any retiring Associate Member nominated for
such membership. Only Active Members are permitted to vote,

Page 2 - Margaret Ellison - February 8, 1991


hold office and serve as members of any standing committee of
the Association.

Active Members of the Association are required to
pay annual dues of $75. Associate Members are required to pay
dues of $350 for the first year of membership and thereafter
annual dues of $250. Life Members and Honorary Members are
not required to pay dues. The Association has no other
sources of funds other than dues. While the membership
changes from year to year, on average approximately 40% of the
members are Active and 60% are Associate.

The dues paid by the Association are used to pay the
costs and expenses associated with holding committee meetings
or the semiannual regular meetings of the membership.
Depending on the circumstances, the Association may pay the
expenses (travel and lodging) of individual Active Members to
attend various functions. Likewise, the funds of the
Association may be used to pay a portion of the costs of
holding a meeting or luncheon and therefore apportioned over
the membership as a whole.

We have concluded that the use of Association funds
to pay certain costs and expenses, whether it be travel,
lodging or meals, is a gift to the Active Members within the
meaning of Section 82008 of the Political Reform Act of 1974.
The question which the Association is seeking advice on is
whether the gift is a gift from the Association, or a gift
from the Associates Members to the extent a portion of the
funds used to pay for the gift was derived from the dues paid
by Associate Members.

If you require further information concerning this
matter, please feel free to contact me directly.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
Jan M. Brockman
for O'MELVENY & MYERS

JMB:lcv961200KT

embedding.qa_text: February 8th, 1991 Margaret Ellison Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: California Association of County Treasurers and Tax Collectors Dear Ms. Ellison: On behalf of the California Association of County Treasurers and Tax Collectors (the "Association"), we are seeking your advice with respect to the characterization of certain gifts made by the Association from Association funds to its members who are county treasurers and tax collectors. The Association is a 501(c)(6) organization under the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended. The purpose of the Association, as provided in its Articles of Association, is to promote the general interests of the Active Members and the respective Counties they represent, to strive for higher professional standards and, through the exchange of information and ideas, stimulate a friendly and cooperative spirit among the membership. The Association has four classes of membership: (1) Active Members - any duly elected or appointed County Treasurer and/or Tax Collector within the State of California; (2) Associate Members - any officer, partner, manager or representative of a firm having an active interest in and ability to contribute to the purposes of the Association; (3) Life Members - any retired Active Member elected for such membership by a majority vote of the members; and (4) Honorary Members - any retiring Associate Member nominated for such membership. Only Active Members are permitted to vote, hold office and serve as members of any standing committee of the Association. Active Members of the Association are required to pay annual dues of $75. Associate Members are required to pay dues of $350 for the first year of membership and thereafter annual dues of $250. Life Members and Honorary Members are not required to pay dues. The Association has no other sources of funds other than dues. While the membership changes from year to year, on average approximately 40% of the members are Active and 60% are Associate. The dues paid by the Association are used to pay the costs and expenses associated with holding committee meetings or the semiannual regular meetings of the membership. Depending on the circumstances, the Association may pay the expenses (travel and lodging) of individual Active Members to attend various functions. Likewise, the funds of the Association may be used to pay a portion of the costs of holding a meeting or luncheon and therefore apportioned over the membership as a whole. We have concluded that the use of Association funds to pay certain costs and expenses, whether it be travel, lodging or meals, is a gift to the Active Members within the meaning of Section 82028 of the Political Reform Act of 1974. The question which the Association is seeking advice on is whether the gift is a gift from the Association, or a gift from the Associate Members to the extent a portion of the funds used to pay for the gift was derived from the dues paid by Associate Members.
